attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks to use the Claude API, Anthropic SDKs, or Managed Agents (`/v1/agents`, `/v1/sessions`); user asks to add, modify, debug, optimize, or improve a Claude feature (prompt caching, cache hit rate, adaptive thinking, compaction, code_execution, batch, files API, citations, memory tool) or a Claude model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; or user asks about prompt caching / cache hit rate / cache reads / cache creation in any project that uses the Anthropic SDK (even without mentioning Claude by name).
DO NOT TRIGGER when: file imports `openai`/non-Anthropic SDK, filename signals another provider (`agent-openai.py`, `*-generic.py`), code is provider-neutral, or task is general programming/ML.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### Schema example
237	
238	Stdin:
239	
240	```json
241	{
242	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
243	  "steps": [
244	    {
245	      "op": "quote",
246	      "items": [
247	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
248	      ]
249	    },
250	    {
251	      "op": "claim",
252	      "policy": 0,
253	      "incident": {
254	        "cause": "fire",
255	        "damages": [
256	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
257	        ]
258	      }
259	    }
260	  ]
261	}
262	```
263	
264	Stdout shape:
265	
266	```json
267	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
268	```
269	
270	## Expected Output Files
271	
272	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
273	- Test files
274	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
275	
276	## Constraints
277	
278	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
279	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
280	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
281	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
282

file.content:
# MHPCO Claim Office Kata

## Setting

You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
its venerable traditions.

## Feature

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
damage report against an existing policy.

## Rules

### Item values and base premiums

Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
the MHPCO price list:

- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.

### Premium modifiers

- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
  surcharge.
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
  20 % loyalty discount.
- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.

All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.

### Claim processing

A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.

- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
  the damage amount.
- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.

## Examples

The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
modifiers, unless stated otherwise.

### Building block of 3 alike components

- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium

### "Alike" components

> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?

- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

### Modifier scope on multi-item policies

> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?

- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
  added at the very end

### Modifier thresholds

- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
  surcharge applies only if cursed
- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

### Deductible per damage event

- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
  per damaged item)

### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)

- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)

### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material

- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
  then deductible: 800 − 100)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
  deductible: 500 − 100)

### Multiple items of the same type

> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
> two?

- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
  cap 4000 G
- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
  separate damage with its own deductible
- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
  whole claim is rejected

### Cap exhaustion

- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
  only, not the insurance sum
- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
  claims of 1500 G each
- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
  is reduced to the remaining cap)

### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor

- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
  (rounded up)
- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
  (rounded down)
- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
  only the final premium or payout is rounded

### Edge cases

- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
  to stdout
- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr
- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
  stderr

## Integration examples

The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.

### Newcomer with a cursed sword

- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- premium: 165 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)

### Long-standing customer's second contract

> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
> insurance surcharge still apply?

- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
  in the scenario
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- premium: 160 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
  = 160 G)
- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
  history.

## CLI input/output format

The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
stdout.

A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.

Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:

- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
  claim).

### Schema example

Stdin:

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Stdout shape:

```json
{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
```

## Expected Output Files

- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
- Test files
- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)

## Constraints

- Use TypeScript for the implementation
- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
- Internal data structures and module organization are free

1	---
2	name: test-list
3	description: "TDD Test List creator - creates comprehensive test lists using it.todo() covering all spec examples before implementation."
4	color: yellow
5	---
6	
7	You are a TDD Test List specialist with deep knowledge of Test-Driven Development, test case planning, and systematic feature decomposition into testable units.
8	
9	## Your Mission
10	
11	Create a comprehensive test list that covers **every example and rule** from the specification:
12	1. Read `prompt.md` thoroughly — every rule, every example, every clarifying question (❓)
13	2. Turn each example into at least one `it.todo()` test case
14	3. Order tests from simplest to most complex
15	4. Use `it.todo()` only — no executable tests yet
16	
17	## TDD Workflow Context
18	The test list is **Step 1** of TDD:
19	1. **Test List** (this agent) - Create test cases with `it.todo()`
20	2. **Red Phase** (/red agent) - Activate one test, make it fail
21	3. **Green Phase** (/green agent) - Minimal implementation
22	4. **Refactor Phase** (/refactor agent) - Improve code
23	5. **Repeat** from step 2 for next test

25	## Context: $ARGUMENTS

28	
29	### Step 1: Understand the Feature
30	Read the complete `prompt.md` specification. Pay special attention to
31	integration examples and clarifying questions (marked with ❓) — these
32	disambiguate rules that may seem open to interpretation in isolation.
33	- What are all operations the system must support?

35	- Which examples in the spec illustrate these rules?

40	`example-mapping/<feature>.md` (create the directory if missing — `<feature>`
41	matches the implementation file basename, e.g. `game-of-life` for
42	`src/game-of-life.ts`). Structure the file as:
43	
44	- **Rules** — the abstract rules from the spec
45	- **Examples** — concrete examples illustrating each rule, with expected values
46	- **Questions / Clarifications** — open or resolved ambiguities (❓ items)
47	- **Per-test rationale** — for each `it.todo()` you will create in Step 4,
48	  one line explaining which rule or example it covers
49	
50	This file is the shared spec memory for the Red and Green subagents, who do
51	not see `prompt.md`. Be thorough but concise: a colleague reading only this
52	file should understand what to build and why.
53	
54	### Step 2: Identify Test Cases from the Spec
55	Walk through the specification section by section. For each rule and each example:
56	- Create a test case that verifies the described behavior
57	- Include the **expected numeric values** from the spec in the test description
58	- If a clarifying question (❓) resolves an ambiguity, create a test for the clarified interpretation
59	- If the spec uses an example-mapping format (rules, examples, questions), every listed example must have a corresponding test
60	
61	**Every requirement in the spec MUST produce at least one test.** This
62	includes:
63	- Every operation the spec names (each gets its own tests)
64	- Every input/output interface the spec describes (e.g. if the spec
65	  mandates a CLI reading from stdin, the test list must include tests
66	  that exercise that CLI surface)
67	- Every error condition the spec calls out (e.g. non-zero exit codes,
68	  malformed input)
69	- Every example, every rule, every ❓ clarification
70	
71	**You MUST NOT defer, postpone, or mark as "out of scope" any
72	requirement that appears in the spec.** Words like "deferred",
73	"end-to-end tests later", "out of scope for now", "skipped for this
74	iteration" must not appear in the test list or the per-test rationale.
75	If the spec asks for it, it gets an `it.todo()`. If you find yourself
76	wanting to defer, that is the strongest signal it belongs in the list.
77	
78	### Step 3: Order Tests (Simple → Complex)
79	Arrange tests in increasing complexity:
80	1. Simplest case (often empty/zero/single item)
81	2. Individual rules in isolation
82	3. Rules with modifiers
83	4. Combinations of multiple rules
84	5. Multi-step scenarios (e.g., operations that reference earlier results)
85	
86	### Step 4: Write Test Descriptions
87	For each test case:
88	- Use `it.todo("description")`
89	- Include expected values: `"should return 115 G (100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee)"`
90	- Be specific and unambiguous
91	- Reference the rule being tested
92	
93	### Step 5: Review Test List
94	Check for:
95	- ✅ Every spec example is covered by at least one test
96	- ✅ Every operation described in the spec has tests
97	- ✅ Every input/output interface the spec mandates has tests (e.g. CLI
98	  surface if the spec requires one)
99	- ✅ Every error condition the spec calls out has a test
100	- ✅ Every clarifying question (❓) has a corresponding test
101	- ✅ Tests are ordered simple → complex
102	- ✅ Each test is independent
103	- ✅ Descriptions include expected values
104	- ✅ All tests use `it.todo()`
105	- ✅ `example-mapping/<feature>.md` exists with Rules / Examples / Questions / Per-test rationale sections
106	- ✅ The test list contains zero deferred / out-of-scope / "later"
107	  entries — every spec requirement has at least one `it.todo()`
108	
109	## Output Format
110	
111	```typescript
112	import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
113	import { functionName } from "./[feature-name].js";
114	
115	describe("Feature Name", () => {
116	  it.todo("should [expected behavior] — [expected value]");
117	  it.todo("should [next behavior] — [expected value]");
118	  // ... ordered simple → complex, covering ALL spec examples
119	});
120	```
121	
122	### Test List Summary
123	After creating test list, provide summary:
124	```
125	📋 Test List Created:
126	**Feature**: [feature name]
127	**Test File**: [filename].spec.ts
128	**Tests**: [count]
129	
130	**Test Cases** (ordered simple → complex):
131	1. ✅ [first test description]
132	2. ✅ [second test description]
133	...
134	
135	**Next Step**: Proceed to Red phase to activate the first test.
136	```
137	
138	## Red Flags
139	
140	Watch for these issues:
141	- Tests ordered randomly (not simple → complex)
142	- Vague or unclear test descriptions
143	- Tests depending on each other
144	- Writing executable tests instead of `it.todo()`
145	- Thinking about implementation instead of behavior
146	- Missing an entire operation described in the spec
147	- Deferring any spec requirement to "later" / "next iteration" / "out
148	  of scope" — every requirement gets an `it.todo()` now
149	- Skipping interface-level tests (CLI/stdin/stdout/exit codes) because
150	  they feel like integration tests — if the spec mandates them, they
151	  belong in the list
152	
153	## Remember
154	
155	- **Cover every spec example** - All rules, all examples, all ❓ clarifications
156	- **`it.todo()` for all tests** - No executable tests yet
157	- **Simple → complex** - Order matters
158	- **Clear descriptions** - Include expected values
159	- **Independent tests** - No dependencies
160	- **No implementation** - Focus on "what", not "how"
161	
162	Your goal is to create a comprehensive, well-ordered test list that covers all specified behavior and sets up the developer for successful TDD workflow.

file.content:
---
name: test-list
description: "TDD Test List creator - creates comprehensive test lists using it.todo() covering all spec examples before implementation."
color: yellow
---

You are a TDD Test List specialist with deep knowledge of Test-Driven Development, test case planning, and systematic feature decomposition into testable units.

## Your Mission

Create a comprehensive test list that covers **every example and rule** from the specification:
1. Read `prompt.md` thoroughly — every rule, every example, every clarifying question (❓)
2. Turn each example into at least one `it.todo()` test case
3. Order tests from simplest to most complex
4. Use `it.todo()` only — no executable tests yet

## TDD Workflow Context
The test list is **Step 1** of TDD:
1. **Test List** (this agent) - Create test cases with `it.todo()`
2. **Red Phase** (/red agent) - Activate one test, make it fail
3. **Green Phase** (/green agent) - Minimal implementation
4. **Refactor Phase** (/refactor agent) - Improve code
5. **Repeat** from step 2 for next test

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Test List Creation Process

### Step 1: Understand the Feature
Read the complete `prompt.md` specification. Pay special attention to
integration examples and clarifying questions (marked with ❓) — these
disambiguate rules that may seem open to interpretation in isolation.
- What are all operations the system must support?
- What rules govern each operation?
- Which examples in the spec illustrate these rules?

### Step 1b: Persist Example Mapping (MUST)

Before writing the test list, condense your understanding of the spec into
`example-mapping/<feature>.md` (create the directory if missing — `<feature>`
matches the implementation file basename, e.g. `game-of-life` for
`src/game-of-life.ts`). Structure the file as:

- **Rules** — the abstract rules from the spec
- **Examples** — concrete examples illustrating each rule, with expected values
- **Questions / Clarifications** — open or resolved ambiguities (❓ items)
- **Per-test rationale** — for each `it.todo()` you will create in Step 4,
  one line explaining which rule or example it covers

This file is the shared spec memory for the Red and Green subagents, who do
not see `prompt.md`. Be thorough but concise: a colleague reading only this
file should understand what to build and why.

### Step 2: Identify Test Cases from the Spec
Walk through the specification section by section. For each rule and each example:
- Create a test case that verifies the described behavior
- Include the **expected numeric values** from the spec in the test description
- If a clarifying question (❓) resolves an ambiguity, create a test for the clarified interpretation
- If the spec uses an example-mapping format (rules, examples, questions), every listed example must have a corresponding test

**Every requirement in the spec MUST produce at least one test.** This
includes:
- Every operation the spec names (each gets its own tests)
- Every input/output interface the spec describes (e.g. if the spec
  mandates a CLI reading from stdin, the test list must include tests
  that exercise that CLI surface)
- Every error condition the spec calls out (e.g. non-zero exit codes,
  malformed input)
- Every example, every rule, every ❓ clarification

**You MUST NOT defer, postpone, or mark as "out of scope" any
requirement that appears in the spec.** Words like "deferred",
"end-to-end tests later", "out of scope for now", "skipped for this
iteration" must not appear in the test list or the per-test rationale.
If the spec asks for it, it gets an `it.todo()`. If you find yourself
wanting to defer, that is the strongest signal it belongs in the list.

### Step 3: Order Tests (Simple → Complex)
Arrange tests in increasing complexity:
1. Simplest case (often empty/zero/single item)
2. Individual rules in isolation
3. Rules with modifiers
4. Combinations of multiple rules
5. Multi-step scenarios (e.g., operations that reference earlier results)

### Step 4: Write Test Descriptions
For each test case:
- Use `it.todo("description")`
- Include expected values: `"should return 115 G (100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee)"`
- Be specific and unambiguous
- Reference the rule being tested

### Step 5: Review Test List
Check for:
- ✅ Every spec example is covered by at least one test
- ✅ Every operation described in the spec has tests
- ✅ Every input/output interface the spec mandates has tests (e.g. CLI
  surface if the spec requires one)
- ✅ Every error condition the spec calls out has a test
- ✅ Every clarifying question (❓) has a corresponding test
- ✅ Tests are ordered simple → complex
- ✅ Each test is independent
- ✅ Descriptions include expected values
- ✅ All tests use `it.todo()`
- ✅ `example-mapping/<feature>.md` exists with Rules / Examples / Questions / Per-test rationale sections
- ✅ The test list contains zero deferred / out-of-scope / "later"
  entries — every spec requirement has at least one `it.todo()`

## Output Format

```typescript
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import { functionName } from "./[feature-name].js";

describe("Feature Name", () => {
  it.todo("should [expected behavior] — [expected value]");
  it.todo("should [next behavior] — [expected value]");
  // ... ordered simple → complex, covering ALL spec examples
});
```

### Test List Summary
After creating test list, provide summary:
```
📋 Test List Created:
**Feature**: [feature name]
**Test File**: [filename].spec.ts
**Tests**: [count]

**Test Cases** (ordered simple → complex):
1. ✅ [first test description]
2. ✅ [second test description]
...

**Next Step**: Proceed to Red phase to activate the first test.
```

## Red Flags

Watch for these issues:
- Tests ordered randomly (not simple → complex)
- Vague or unclear test descriptions
- Tests depending on each other
- Writing executable tests instead of `it.todo()`
- Thinking about implementation instead of behavior
- Missing an entire operation described in the spec
- Deferring any spec requirement to "later" / "next iteration" / "out
  of scope" — every requirement gets an `it.todo()` now
- Skipping interface-level tests (CLI/stdin/stdout/exit codes) because
  they feel like integration tests — if the spec mandates them, they
  belong in the list

## Remember

- **Cover every spec example** - All rules, all examples, all ❓ clarifications
- **`it.todo()` for all tests** - No executable tests yet
- **Simple → complex** - Order matters
- **Clear descriptions** - Include expected values
- **Independent tests** - No dependencies
- **No implementation** - Focus on "what", not "how"

Your goal is to create a comprehensive, well-ordered test list that covers all specified behavior and sets up the developer for successful TDD workflow.

Supports two operations: quote (compute premium) and claim (process damage). content.text:
Here is the summary of what was created.

---

**Test List Created:**

**Feature**: MHPCO Claim Office (quote and claim operations)
**Test File**: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_16-06-05_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-6-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`
**Example Mapping**: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_16-06-05_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-6-portkey-no-thinking/example-mapping/claim-office.md`
**Tests**: 49

**Test Cases** (ordered simple to complex):

**Quote - base premiums (6 tests)**
1. should return 5 G premium for an empty item list (processing fee only)
2. should return 115 G for a single plain sword -- new customer first quote (100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee)
3. should return 71 G for a single plain amulet -- new customer first quote (60 base + 6 first-insurance + 5 fee)
4. should return 93 G for a single plain staff -- new customer first quote (80 base + 8 first-insurance + 5 fee)
5. should return 49 G for a single plain potion -- new customer first quote (40 base + 4 first-insurance + 5 fee)
6. should return 33 G for a single rune -- new customer first quote (25 base + 2.5 first-insurance + 5 fee = 32.5, ceiling 33)

**Quote - component building blocks (4 tests)**
7. should use 50 G base premium for 2 runes (2 x 25, no block)
8. should use 60 G base premium for 3 runes (block of exactly 3 alike)
9. should use 100 G base premium for 4 runes (no block -- block requires exactly 3)
10. should use 175 G base premium for 7 runes (7 x 25, no block)

**Quote - alike components (2 tests)**
11. should use 75 G base premium for 2 runes + 1 moonstone (no block: different types)
12. should use 120 G base premium for 3 runes + 3 moonstones (two separate blocks: 60 + 60)

**Quote - item-specific modifiers (4 tests)**
13. should add 50% curse surcharge on a cursed sword's base premium
14. should add 30% high-enchantment surcharge for sword with enchantment exactly 5
15. should not add high-enchantment surcharge for sword with enchantment 4
16. should apply both cursed and high-enchantment surcharges when sword is cursed with enchantment >= 5

**Quote - modifier scope on multi-item policies (1 test)**
17. should apply cursed surcharge only to the cursed item's base premium -- cursed sword + plain amulet

**Quote - policy-wide modifiers (4 tests)**
18. should apply 20% loyalty discount for customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO
19. should not apply loyalty discount for customer with 1 year with MHPCO
20. should apply 15% follow-up discount on second and subsequent quotes
21. should always apply 10% first-insurance surcharge to every item regardless of customer history

**Quote - rounding (1 test)**
22. should round premium up (ceiling) in MHPCO's favor -- e.g. 197.5 G becomes 198 G

**Quote - integration (2 tests)**
23. should return 165 G for newcomer with cursed sword (E11)
24. should return 160 G for long-standing customer second contract (E12)

**Claim - standard reimbursement (2 tests)**
25. should return payout 400 G for regular steel sword ench 3 with 500 G damage
26. should return payout 100 G for rune with 200 G damage

**Claim - enchantment and material clauses (4 tests)**
27. should return payout 400 G for dragon-material sword ench 9 with 1000 G damage (both clauses)
28. should return payout 700 G for dragon-material sword ench 5 with 800 G damage (dragon only)
29. should return payout 400 G for steel sword ench 9 with 1000 G damage (high-ench only)
30. should return payout 400 G for dragon-material sword ench exactly 8 with 1000 G damage (threshold)

**Claim - deductible (1 test)**
31. should apply 100 G deductible per damaged item -- sword 500 + amulet 300 = 600

**Claim - multiple items of same type (3 tests)**
32. should compute insurance sum 2000 G and cap 4000 G for two swords
33. should treat two sword damage entries each with its own deductible
34. should reject claim when damages exceed insured item count for a type

**Claim - cap (3 tests)**
35. should compute cap as 2x insurance sum -- sword + amulet: cap 3200 G
36. should base cap on unmodified insurance value -- cursed sword: cap 2000 G
37. should compute insurance sum including components at 250 G each -- sword + 3 runes: 1750 G

**Claim - cap exhaustion (2 tests)**
38. should return payout 1400 G and remainingCap 600 G on first claim of 1500 G
39. should return payout 600 G and remainingCap 0 G on second claim when 600 G cap remains

**Claim - rounding (1 test)**
40. should round payout down (floor) in MHPCO's favor -- e.g. 350.5 G becomes 350 G

**Edge cases and errors (4 tests)**
41. should exit with non-zero status for unknown item type in quote
42. should exit with non-zero status when claim references item not in policy
43. should exit with non-zero status for negative damage amount
44. should exit with non-zero status when claim references unknown item type

**CLI interface (5 tests)**
45. should read JSON scenario from stdin and write JSON results to stdout
46. should produce results array of same length and order as input steps
47. should output quote result with integer premium field
48. should output claim result with integer payout and remainingCap fields
49. should exit with non-zero status code and write to stderr on error -- no results to stdout

**Next Step**: Proceed to Red phase to activate the first test.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office -- Example Mapping
2	
3	## Rules
4	
5	### R1 -- Item values and base premiums
6	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
7	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
8	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
9	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
10	- Component (rune, moonstone, etc.): 250 G insurance value, 25 G base premium each
11	- Building block of exactly 3 alike components: 60 G base premium (instead of 75 G)
12	
13	### R2 -- Premium modifiers (applied in a specific order)
14	- **Item-specific modifiers** (applied per item to that item's base premium):
15	  - Cursed: +50 % of that item's base premium
16	  - Highly enchanted (enchantment >= 5): +30 % of that item's base premium
17	  - First insurance: +10 % of that item's base premium (every item in a quote is treated as first insurance, regardless of customer history)
18	- **Policy-wide modifiers** (applied to the policy base premium = sum of all item base premiums):
19	  - Loyalty (customer >= 2 years with MHPCO): -20 % of policy base premium
20	  - Follow-up contract (each contract after the customer's first quote): -15 % of policy base premium
21	- **Processing fee**: +5 G flat, added at the very end
22	
23	**Modifier stacking computation** (from integration examples):
24	- Total premium = sum(item base premiums) + sum(item-specific surcharges) + sum(policy-wide adjustments) + 5 G fee
25	- Item-specific surcharges are percentages of the individual item's base premium
26	- Policy-wide modifiers are percentages of the policy base premium (sum of all item base premiums before any surcharges)
27	- First insurance surcharge (+10%) is applied to each item's base premium (treated as item-specific)
28	- Rounding: only the final premium (after fee) is rounded to whole G in MHPCO's favor (rounded up / ceiling)
29	
30	### R3 -- Claim processing
31	- Deductible: 100 G per damaged item (per damage entry, not per event)
32	- Cap: 2x the insurance sum (sum of all insured items' insurance values)
33	- Cap is based on unmodified insurance values (premium modifiers do not raise it)
34	- The block discount on components affects premium only, not insurance sum

40	- Neither clause: full reimbursement (then deductible)

48	- Premium: round UP (ceiling) to whole G

file.content:
# MHPCO Claim Office -- Example Mapping

## Rules

### R1 -- Item values and base premiums
- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
- Component (rune, moonstone, etc.): 250 G insurance value, 25 G base premium each
- Building block of exactly 3 alike components: 60 G base premium (instead of 75 G)

### R2 -- Premium modifiers (applied in a specific order)
- **Item-specific modifiers** (applied per item to that item's base premium):
  - Cursed: +50 % of that item's base premium
  - Highly enchanted (enchantment >= 5): +30 % of that item's base premium
  - First insurance: +10 % of that item's base premium (every item in a quote is treated as first insurance, regardless of customer history)
- **Policy-wide modifiers** (applied to the policy base premium = sum of all item base premiums):
  - Loyalty (customer >= 2 years with MHPCO): -20 % of policy base premium
  - Follow-up contract (each contract after the customer's first quote): -15 % of policy base premium
- **Processing fee**: +5 G flat, added at the very end

**Modifier stacking computation** (from integration examples):
- Total premium = sum(item base premiums) + sum(item-specific surcharges) + sum(policy-wide adjustments) + 5 G fee
- Item-specific surcharges are percentages of the individual item's base premium
- Policy-wide modifiers are percentages of the policy base premium (sum of all item base premiums before any surcharges)
- First insurance surcharge (+10%) is applied to each item's base premium (treated as item-specific)
- Rounding: only the final premium (after fee) is rounded to whole G in MHPCO's favor (rounded up / ceiling)

### R3 -- Claim processing
- Deductible: 100 G per damaged item (per damage entry, not per event)
- Cap: 2x the insurance sum (sum of all insured items' insurance values)
- Cap is based on unmodified insurance values (premium modifiers do not raise it)
- The block discount on components affects premium only, not insurance sum

### R4 -- Claim reimbursement special clauses
- Enchantment >= 8: reimburse at 50 % of damage amount (then deductible)
- Dragon material: fully reimburse (then deductible)
- Both clauses apply: 50 % wins (then deductible)
- Neither clause: full reimbursement (then deductible)
- Components (runes, moonstones) have no enchantment or material; standard reimbursement applies

### R5 -- Cap exhaustion
- Successive claims reduce the remaining cap
- When the computed payout exceeds remaining cap, payout is reduced to remaining cap

### R6 -- Rounding in MHPCO's favor
- Premium: round UP (ceiling) to whole G
- Payout: round DOWN (floor) to whole G
- Intermediate calculations kept as fractions; only final result rounded

### R7 -- Edge cases / errors
- Empty item list: premium = 5 G (only processing fee)
- Unknown item type in quote: non-zero exit code, error to stderr, no results to stdout
- Claim references item not in policy: non-zero exit code, error to stderr
- Claim references unknown item type: non-zero exit code, error to stderr
- Claim damage count exceeds insured count for a type: non-zero exit code, error to stderr
- Negative damage amount: non-zero exit code, error to stderr

### R8 -- CLI interface
- Executable: `claim-office`
- Entry point: `src/cli.ts`
- Reads JSON from stdin with `customer` object and `steps` array
- Writes JSON to stdout with `results` array (same length/order as steps)
- Quote result: `{ premium: <integer> }`
- Claim result: `{ payout: <integer>, remainingCap: <integer> }`
- Steps processed sequentially; claim steps reference earlier quote via zero-based step index in `policy` field

## Examples

### E1 -- Component base premiums
- 2 runes -> 50 G base premium (2 x 25)
- 3 runes -> 60 G base premium (block of 3 alike)
- 4 runes -> 100 G base premium (no block; 4 x 25)
- 7 runes -> 175 G base premium (7 x 25; no block)

### E2 -- "Alike" components
- 2 runes + 1 moonstone -> 75 G (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones -> 120 G (two separate blocks: 60 + 60)

### E3 -- Modifier scope on multi-item policies
- Cursed sword (100 G base) + plain amulet (60 G base) -> policy base 160 G; cursed surcharge 50 G (50% of 100 G only) -> 210 G before further modifiers and fee

### E4 -- Modifier thresholds
- Customer exactly 2 years -> loyalty discount applies
- Sword enchantment exactly 5 -> high-enchantment surcharge applies
- Sword enchantment 5, cursed -> both surcharges apply
- Sword enchantment 4 -> no high-enchantment surcharge
- Dragon-material sword, enchantment exactly 8, damage 1000 G -> payout 400 G (50% = 500, minus 100 deductible)

### E5 -- Deductible per damage entry
- Dragon attack damages sword (500 G) and amulet (300 G) -> payout 600 G (each gets 100 G deductible: 400 + 200)

### E6 -- Standard reimbursement
- Steel sword, enchantment 3, damage 500 G -> payout 400 G (500 - 100)
- Rune, damage 200 G -> payout 100 G (200 - 100)

### E7 -- Enchantment threshold vs dragon material
- Dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G -> payout 400 G (50% = 500, minus 100)
- Dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G -> payout 700 G (full, minus 100: 800 - 100)
- Steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G -> payout 400 G (50% = 500, minus 100)

### E8 -- Multiple items of same type
- Two swords -> insurance sum 2000 G, cap 4000 G
- Two sword damages -> each treated separately with own deductible
- More damages than insured items -> non-zero exit, claim rejected

### E9 -- Cap exhaustion
- Sword + amulet -> insurance sum 1600 G, cap 3200 G
- Cursed sword (value 1000 G) -> cap 2000 G (based on unmodified value)
- Sword + 3 runes -> insurance sum 1750 G (1000 + 3x250); block discount only affects premium
- Sword insured (sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); first claim 1500 G -> payout 1400 G, remaining 600 G; second claim 1500 G -> payout 600 G, remaining 0 G

### E10 -- Rounding
- Premium yielding 197.5 G -> 198 G (ceiling)
- Payout yielding 350.5 G -> 350 G (floor)

### E11 -- Integration: Newcomer with cursed sword
- Customer: 0 years, no previous contract
- Item: cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- Premium: 165 G = 100 base + 50 curse + 10 first insurance + 5 fee

### E12 -- Integration: Long-standing customer's second contract
- Customer: 3 years, second quote
- Item: cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- Premium: 160 G = 100 base + 50 curse + 30 high-enchantment - 20 loyalty + 10 first insurance - 15 follow-up + 5 fee = 155 + 5

### E13 -- Edge cases
- Empty item list -> premium 5 G
- Unknown item type in quote -> error exit
- Claim references item not in policy -> error exit
- Negative damage amount -> error exit

## Questions / Clarifications

### Q1 -- "Alike" components (resolved)
"Alike" means exactly the same type (e.g., all runes). Different component types (runes vs moonstones) do not form a block together.

### Q2 -- Modifier scope (resolved)
Item-specific modifiers (cursed, high-enchantment, first insurance) apply to the individual item's base premium. Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, follow-up) apply to the policy base premium (sum of all item base premiums).

### Q3 -- "First insurance" meaning (resolved)
"First insurance" applies to every item in every quote -- each item is always treated as a first insurance. It is NOT about whether the customer has had a previous contract. The "follow-up contract" discount is the one that relates to customer history (second and subsequent quotes).

## Per-test Rationale

Each line maps one `it.todo()` in `src/claim-office.spec.ts` to the rule(s) and example(s) it covers.

### Quote - base premiums
1. "should return 5 G premium for an empty item list (processing fee only)" -- E13/R1/R2: empty list, only 5 G fee
2. "should return 115 G for a single plain sword" -- R1/R2: 100 base + 10 first-ins (10% of 100) + 5 fee
3. "should return 71 G for a single plain amulet" -- R1/R2: 60 base + 6 first-ins + 5 fee
4. "should return 93 G for a single plain staff" -- R1/R2: 80 base + 8 first-ins + 5 fee
5. "should return 49 G for a single plain potion" -- R1/R2: 40 base + 4 first-ins + 5 fee
6. "should return 33 G for a single rune" -- R1/R2/R6: 25 base + 2.5 first-ins + 5 fee = 32.5 -> ceil = 33

### Quote - component building blocks
7. "2 runes -> 50 G base premium" -- E1: 2 x 25, no block
8. "3 runes -> 60 G base premium (block)" -- E1: block of exactly 3 alike
9. "4 runes -> 100 G base premium (no block)" -- E1: block requires exactly 3
10. "7 runes -> 175 G base premium" -- E1: 7 x 25, no block

### Quote - alike components
11. "2 runes + 1 moonstone -> 75 G base (no block)" -- E2/Q1: different types
12. "3 runes + 3 moonstones -> 120 G base (two blocks)" -- E2/Q1: same-type blocks

### Quote - item-specific modifiers
13. "cursed sword -> +50% of item base" -- R2: curse surcharge
14. "enchantment 5 sword -> +30% high-enchantment" -- R2/E4: threshold at 5
15. "enchantment 4 sword -> no high-enchantment surcharge" -- E4: below threshold
16. "cursed + enchantment >= 5 -> both surcharges" -- E4: stacking

### Quote - modifier scope
17. "cursed sword + plain amulet -> surcharge on sword only" -- E3/Q2: item-specific scope

### Quote - policy-wide modifiers
18. "customer 2 years -> loyalty discount applies" -- R2/E4: threshold at 2
19. "customer 1 year -> no loyalty discount" -- R2: below threshold
20. "second quote -> follow-up discount applies" -- R2: follow-up contract
21. "first insurance always applies per item" -- R2/Q3: regardless of history

### Quote - rounding
22. "premium rounding up (ceiling)" -- R6/E10: e.g. 197.5 -> 198

### Quote - integration
23. "newcomer cursed sword -> 165 G" -- E11: 100 + 50 + 10 + 5
24. "long-standing second contract cursed ench 7 sword -> 160 G" -- E12: 100 + 50 + 30 - 20 + 10 - 15 + 5

### Claim - standard reimbursement
25. "steel sword ench 3, 500 damage -> 400 payout" -- E6: full - 100 deductible
26. "rune, 200 damage -> 100 payout" -- E6: full - 100, no special clause

### Claim - enchantment and material clauses
27. "dragon-material sword ench 9, 1000 damage -> 400 payout" -- E7/R4: both -> 50% wins
28. "dragon-material sword ench 5, 800 damage -> 700 payout" -- E7/R4: dragon only -> full
29. "steel sword ench 9, 1000 damage -> 400 payout" -- E7/R4: high-ench only -> 50%
30. "dragon-material sword ench 8, 1000 damage -> 400 payout" -- E4/R4: threshold at 8

### Claim - deductible
31. "sword 500 + amulet 300 -> 600 payout" -- E5/R3: 100 G deductible per entry

### Claim - multiple items of same type
32. "two swords -> sum 2000, cap 4000" -- E8/R3
33. "two sword damages each with own deductible" -- E8
34. "more damages than insured -> error" -- E8/R7

### Claim - cap
35. "sword + amulet -> cap 3200" -- E9/R3: 2 x 1600
36. "cursed sword cap -> 2000 (unmodified value)" -- E9/R3
37. "sword + 3 runes -> sum 1750" -- E9/R3: block discount premium only

### Claim - cap exhaustion
38. "first claim 1500 -> payout 1400, remaining 600" -- E9/R5
39. "second claim 1500 -> payout 600, remaining 0" -- E9/R5

### Claim - rounding
40. "payout rounding down (floor)" -- R6/E10: e.g. 350.5 -> 350

### Edge cases and errors
41. "unknown item type in quote -> error" -- E13/R7
42. "claim references item not in policy -> error" -- E13/R7
43. "negative damage amount -> error" -- E13/R7
44. "claim references unknown item type -> error" -- R7

### CLI interface
45. "CLI reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON results to stdout" -- R8
46. "results array same length and order as steps" -- R8
47. "quote result has integer premium field" -- R8
48. "claim result has integer payout and remainingCap" -- R8
49. "CLI error: non-zero exit, stderr, no stdout results" -- R8/R7

content.text:
Green Phase Complete:

**Implementation**: Replaced the simple `reduce` sum with a group-by-type loop that counts items per type. When a component type (currently only "rune") has exactly 3 items, the block premium of 60 G is used instead of 3 x 25 = 75 G. Added `COMPONENT_TYPES`, `BLOCK_SIZE`, and `BLOCK_PREMIUM` constants.

**Result**: All tests now pass (8 passing, 41 todo)

**Approach**: This is minimal because: (1) the grouping-by-type is the smallest structural change that enables block detection -- a simple reduce cannot identify groups; (2) the block condition (`count === BLOCK_SIZE` for component types) is exactly what the test demands; (3) no logic for future tests (4 runes, 7 runes, mixed types) was added -- those cases naturally fall through to the per-item calculation.

Proceeding to Refactor phase.

content.text:
Refactor phase complete. Here is a summary of what was done:

**Refactoring applied**: Extracted two new helper functions from inline claim-processing logic in `processScenario`:

1. `calculateInsuranceCap(policyItems: Item[]): number` -- computes the insurance cap as 2x the sum of item insurance values
2. `calculateClaimPayout(damages: Damage[]): number` -- computes total payout from damages after applying the per-damage deductible

**Motivation**: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent). Before this refactoring, the quote branch of `processScenario` delegated to well-named helpers (`calculateBasePremium`, `calculateItemSurcharges`, `calculateLoyaltyDiscount`, `calculateFollowUpDiscount`) while the claim branch had raw inline computation. Extracting these helpers brings both branches to the same abstraction level, making `processScenario` read as a high-level orchestrator for both step types.

**Mass change**: 96 -> 111 (justified increase from helper function signatures; clarity gain outweighs mass cost per Rule 2 priority over APP).

**Tests**: All 25 passing.

The claim processing needs to look at the insured item's enchantment and material properties when computing reimbursement. The quote step's items array has this information and the claim references it via the policy field.

content.text:
Green Phase Complete:

**Implementation**: Added enchantment-based reimbursement clause to `calculateClaimPayout`. The function now accepts `policyItems` as a second parameter, looks up the matching insured item for each damage entry, and applies a 50% reimbursement rate when the item's enchantment is >= 8 (using the new `CLAIM_HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` constant). Also added `material?: string` to the `Item` interface (inert -- no behavior depends on it yet).

**Result**: All tests now pass (27 passing, 22 todo)

**Approach**: This is minimal because:
- Only the enchantment >= 8 check is implemented, which is the single condition that differentiates this test from the previous standard-reimbursement tests. The dragon-material clause is not checked because no test currently exercises the dragon-material-only case (enchantment < 8 with dragon material). Future tests (e.g., "dragon-material sword ench 5, 800 damage -> 700 payout") will force that distinction.
- The `material` property on `Item` is added only as a type annotation since the test data includes it; it drives no behavior.

Proceeding to Refactor phase.

- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_16-06-05_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-6-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts` (implementation)

The Red phase is complete. Here is a summary of what happened:

**Test Activated**: "should return payout 700 G for dragon-material sword ench 5 with 800 G damage (only dragon clause: full, 800 - 100 deductible)"

This test is a triangulation/guard test. The current implementation in `calculateClaimPayout` applies full reimbursement (rate 1.0) whenever the enchantment is below the claim high-enchantment threshold of 8. A dragon-material sword with enchantment 5 falls below that threshold, so it receives full reimbursement: 800 * 1 - 100 = 700 G payout. The cap for one sword is 2000 G, and the remaining cap is 2000 - 700 = 1300 G. The test passed immediately because the default path already produces the correct result, even though dragon material is not explicitly checked yet.

Both predictions were correct. The test passes with 28 tests passing and 21 remaining as todo.

**Implementation**: Added a `policyPayouts` map (keyed by policy step index) in `processScenario` to track cumulative payouts per policy across successive claim steps. The existing `calculateInsuranceCap`, `calculateClaimPayout`, and other helpers were left untouched. The three new lines (`priorPayouts`, `currentCap`, accumulation) are the smallest possible addition to make the second claim respect the exhausted cap.

input.prompt:

content.text:
Green Phase Complete:

**Implementation**: Added `Math.floor` around the payout computation in the claim branch of `processScenario`. The line changed from `const payout = Math.min(totalPayout, currentCap)` to `const payout = Math.floor(Math.min(totalPayout, currentCap))`. The `remainingCap` is then computed from the already-floored payout, so it also comes out as a whole number.

**Result**: All tests now pass (40 passing, 9 todo).

**Approach**: This is minimal because it adds exactly one function call (`Math.floor`) at the single point where the final payout is determined. No other logic was changed. The `remainingCap` naturally gets the correct integer value because it subtracts the already-floored payout from the integer cap.

Proceeding to Refactor phase.

**Relevant files:**
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_16-06-05_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-6-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts` (line 153 -- the modified line)

content.text:
All done. Here's a summary of the completed TDD exercise:

**MHPCO Claim Office** - 49 tests, all passing.

**What was built:**
- `src/claim-office.ts` - Core logic with `processScenario()` handling both quote and claim operations
- `src/cli.ts` - CLI entry point reading JSON from stdin, writing results to stdout
- `src/claim-office.spec.ts` - 49 comprehensive tests covering all spec requirements

**Key features implemented:**
- **Quote operation**: Base premiums for 6 item types, component block discounts (exactly 3 alike), item-specific surcharges (cursed 50%, high-enchantment 30%), policy-wide modifiers (loyalty -20%, follow-up -15%), first-insurance surcharge (+10%), processing fee (+5 G), ceiling rounding
- **Claim operation**: Per-damage deductible (100 G), enchantment >= 8 half reimbursement, dragon material full reimbursement, cap tracking (2x insurance sum) across successive claims, floor rounding
- **Error handling**: Unknown item types, damage count exceeding insured count, negative damage amounts, items not in policy
- **CLI interface**: JSON stdin/stdout, non-zero exit codes with stderr on errors

**TDD cycle stats**: 49 Red-Green-Refactor cycles completed autonomously. Many tests (especially guard/triangulation tests) passed immediately due to well-structured incremental implementation.
